meta.title: Angular DI Guide – Injectors, Providers & Hierarchical Injection (2026)
meta.description: Master the core of Angular's architecture. Learn how Dependency Injection works, how to use @Injectable, and how to manage service hierarchies.

content.value: Dependency Injection is a coding pattern in which a class receives its dependencies from an external provider rather than creating them internally. In Angular, this means your components don't need to know how to instantiate a service or manage its lifecycle; they simply 'ask' for it in their constructor. This promotes the 'Inversion of Control' (IoC) principle, making your code cleaner and easier to test.

To make a class available for injection, you must use the `@Injectable()` decorator. This tells Angular that this class can be managed by the DI system. The `providedIn: 'root'` property ensures the service is a singleton and allows for tree-shaking (removing the code if it's never used).

Angular uses a hierarchical DI system. When a component requests a dependency, Angular first looks at the component's own providers, then its parent's, and so on, until it reaches the root. This allows you to have global singletons or 'sandboxed' service instances that only live within a specific feature module.
In Angular, you 'inject' a dependency by declaring it as a parameter in the class constructor. Angular's Injector looks at the type of the parameter and provides the correct instance.

rows: The object/service that a class needs to function.
The instruction on how to create/obtain the dependency.
The mechanism that finds or creates the dependency.
The decorator that makes a class 'available' for DI.

faq.question: Why use DI instead of just importing the class?
faq.answer: Importing a class only gives you the type. DI gives you the instance. More importantly, DI allows you to swap that instance (e.g., using a MockApiService during tests) without changing any code in your component.

faq.question: What is a Singleton in Angular?
faq.answer: When a service is provided in 'root', Angular creates one single instance for the entire application. Every component that injects that service will share the exact same instance and data.

faq.question: Can I inject things that aren't classes?
faq.answer: Yes. You can use 'InjectionTokens' to inject strings, configurations, or interfaces. This is common for providing API environment URLs or secret keys.

quiz.question: Where do you typically declare that a component needs a specific service?
What does 'providedIn: root' achieve in a service?
options: It makes the service local to one component
It makes the service a singleton and enables tree-shaking
It prevents the service from being used in other modules
It automatically connects the service to a database
